stimulus:
Francis: Failure to become properly registered to vote prevents one-third of the voting-age citizens of Lagonia from voting. Some local election boards have been absorbing this fact by hiring extra help and holding training sessions for staff who help register citizens. However, these measures make the process more cumbersome, not less, and undermine public confidence in the effects of voting.
Sharon: The high number of citizens not registered to vote has persisted despite many attempts by local election boards to simplify the registration process. Thus, failure to register is due to apathy, not to any problems with the registration process.
questionStem: The main issue in dispute between Francis and Sharon is

answerChoices.A: whether changing the voter registration process would be cumbersome
answerChoices.B: why so many citizens do not register to vote
answerChoices.C: what percentage of those registered to vote actually vote
answerChoices.D: whether local election boards have simplified the registration process
answerChoices.E: why the public lacks confidence in the effects of voting

stimulus: Advertisement: Anyone who thinks moisturizers are not important for beautiful skin should consider what happens to the earth, the skin of the world, in times of drought. Without regular infusions of moisture the ground becomes lined and cracked and its lush loveliness fades away. Thus your skin, too, should be protected from the ravages caused by lack of moisture; give it the protection provided by regular infusions of Dewyfresh, the drought-defying moisturizer.
questionStem: The Dewyfresh advertisement exhibits which one of the following errors of reasoning?

answerChoices.A: It treats something that is necessary for bringing about a state of affairs as something that is sufficient to bring about that state of affairs.
answerChoices.B: It treats the fact that two things regularly occur together as proof that there is a single thing that is the cause of them both.
answerChoices.C: It overlooks the fact that changing what people think is the case does not necessarily change what is the case.
answerChoices.D: It relies on the ambiguity of the term “infusion,” which can designate either a process or the product of that process.
answerChoices.E: It relies on an analogy between two things that are insufficiently alike in the respects in which they would have to be alike for the conclusion to be supported.

stimulus:
M: The Greek alphabet must have been invented by some individual who knew the Phoenician writing system and who wanted to have some way of recording Homeric epics and thereby preserving expressions of a highly developed tradition of oral poetry.
P: Your hypothesis is laughable! What would have been the point of such a person’s writing Homeric epics down? Surely a person who knew them well enough to write them down would not need to read them; and no one else could read them, according to your hypothesis.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an argumentative strategy that P uses in responding to M?

answerChoices.A: attacking M’s understanding of the literary value of oral poetry
answerChoices.B: disagreeing with M’s thesis without attempting to refute it
answerChoices.C: challenging M’s knowledge of the Phoenician writing system
answerChoices.D: attempting to undermine M’s hypothesis by making it appear absurd
answerChoices.E: providing an alternative interpretation of evidence put forward by M

stimulus:
M: The Greek alphabet must have been invented by some individual who knew the Phoenician writing system and who wanted to have some way of recording Homeric epics and thereby preserving expressions of a highly developed tradition of oral poetry.
P: Your hypothesis is laughable! What would have been the point of such a person’s writing Homeric epics down? Surely a person who knew them well enough to write
them down would not need to read them; and no one else could read them, according to your hypothesis.
questionStem: P’s argument is vulnerable to which one of the following criticisms?

answerChoices.A: It fails to demonstrate that the Phoenician alphabet alone could have provided the basis for the Greek alphabet.
answerChoices.B: It incorrectly assumes that the first text ever written in Greek was a Homeric poem.
answerChoices.C: It confuses the requirements for a complex oral tradition with the requirements of a written language.
answerChoices.D: It attempts to demonstrate the truth of a hypothesis merely by showing that it is possible.
answerChoices.E: It overlooks the possibility that the person who invented the Greek alphabet did so with the intention of teaching it to others.

stimulus: Bacteria from food can survive for several days on the surface of plastic cutting boards, but bacteria can penetrate wooden cutting boards almost immediately, leaving the surface free of contamination. Therefore, wooden cutting boards, unlike plastic cutting boards, need not be washed in order to prevent their contaminating food that is cut on them; wiping them off to remove food debris is sufficient.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?

answerChoices.A: Washing plastic cutting boards does not remove all bacteria from the surface.
answerChoices.B: Prevention of bacterial contamination is the only respect in which wooden cutting boards are superior to plastic cutting boards.
answerChoices.C: Food that is not already contaminated with bacteria can be contaminated only by being cut on contaminated cutting boards.
answerChoices.D: Bacteria that penetrate into wooden cutting boards do not reemerge on the surface after the cutting boards have been used.
answerChoices.E: Washing wooden cutting boards kills bacteria below the surface of the cutting boards.

stimulus: Asthmagon was long considered the most effective of the drugs known as beta-2 agonists, designed to alleviate asthma attacks. However, studies conducted in Rhiago between 1981 and 1987 revealed that nearly one out of every five of the asthma patients under observation who took asthmagon suffered serious side effects after taking the drug. Citing this statistic, some doctors argue that asthmagon should be banned as an anti-asthma drug.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if true, most weakens the case for the proposed ban of asthmagon?

answerChoices.A: In Rhiago, where asthmagon had been the most widely prescribed of the beta-2 agonists, the number of asthma deaths increased between 1981 and 1987.
answerChoices.B: Many of the patients under observation to whom asthmagon was administered had not previously taken a beta-2 agonist.
answerChoices.C: Despite the growing concern about the drug, many physicians in Rhiago still prescribe asthmagon to asthma sufferers.
answerChoices.D: Among the patients observed, only those who had very high cholesterol counts suffered side effects after taking asthmagon.
answerChoices.E: Asthmagon increases the severity of asthma attacks in some people because the drug can cause damage to heart tissues.

stimulus:
In response to requests made by the dairy industry the government is considering whether to approve the synthetic hormone BST for use in dairy cows. BST increases milk production but also leads to recurring udder inflammation, decreased fertility, and symptoms of stress in cows who receive the hormone. All of these problems can be kept under control with constant veterinary care, but such levels of veterinary
help would cost big farms far less per cow than they would small farms.
questionStem: If the statements above are true, which one of the following claims is most strongly supported by them?

answerChoices.A: The government is unlikely to approve the synthetic hormone BST for use in cows.
answerChoices.B: The proportion of cows that suffer from udder inflammation, decreased fertility, and symptoms of stress is currently greater on big dairy farms than on small ones.
answerChoices.C: At the present time milk from cows raised on small farms is safer to drink than milk from cows raised on big farms.
answerChoices.D: The milk from cows who receive BST will not be safe for people to drink.
answerChoices.E: Owners of big farms stand to gain more from government approval of BST than do owners of small farms.

stimulus: Jones is selling a house to Smith. The contract between the two specifies that for up to a year after ownership is transferred, Jones will be responsible for repairing any “major structural defects,” defined as defects in the roof or roof-supporting components of the house, that might be found. Jones is not responsible for any other repairs. The house has a truss roof, which means that the only walls that support the roof are the exterior walls.
questionStem: It can be properly concluded from the information above that

answerChoices.A: Jones did not know of any defects in the roof or roof-supporting components of the house at the time the contract was written
answerChoices.B: although other components of the house may contain defects, the roof and roof-supporting components of the house are currently free from such defects
answerChoices.C: the contract does not oblige Jones to repair any defects in the house’s nonexterior walls after ownership of the house has been transferred
answerChoices.D: Smith will be obliged to repair all structural defects in the house within a year after ownership is transferred, except those for which Jones is responsible
answerChoices.E: in the past Jones has had to make repairs to some of the house’s exterior walls

stimulus: The play Mankind must have been written between 1431 and 1471. It cannot have been written before 1431, for in that year the rose noble, a coin mentioned in the play, was first circulated. The play cannot have been written after 1471, since in that year King Henry VI died, and he is mentioned as a living monarch in the play’s dedication.
questionStem: The argument would be most seriously weakened if which one of the following were discovered?

answerChoices.A: The Royal Theatre Company includes the play on a list of those performed in 1480.
answerChoices.B: Another coin mentioned in the play was first minted in 1422.
answerChoices.C: The rose noble was neither minted nor circulated after 1468.
answerChoices.D: Although Henry VI was deposed in 1461, he was briefly restored to the throne in 1470.
answerChoices.E: In a letter written in early 1428, a merchant told of having seen the design for a much-discussed new coin called the “rose noble.”

stimulus: All material bodies are divisible into parts, and everything divisible is imperfect. It follows that all material bodies are imperfect. It likewise follows that the spirit is not a material body.
questionStem: The final conclusion above follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

answerChoices.A: Everything divisible is a material body.
answerChoices.B: Nothing imperfect is indivisible.
answerChoices.C: The spirit is divisible.
answerChoices.D: The spirit is perfect.
answerChoices.E: The spirit is either indivisible or imperfect.

stimulus: Special kinds of cotton that grow fibers of green or brown have been around since the 1930s but only recently became commercially feasible when a long-fibered variety that can be spun by machine was finally bred. Since the cotton need not be dyed, processing plants avoid the expense of dyeing and the ecological hazards of getting rid of leftover dye and by-products.
questionStem: Which one of the following can be properly inferred from the passage?

answerChoices.A: It is ecologically safer to process long-fibered cotton than short-fibered cotton.
answerChoices.B: Green and brown cottons that can be spun only by hand are not commercially viable.
answerChoices.C: Hand-spun cotton is more ecologically safe than machine-spun cotton.
answerChoices.D: Short-fibered regular cottons are economically competitive with synthetic fabrics.
answerChoices.E: Garments made of green and brown cottons are less expensive than garments made of regular cotton.

stimulus: People in the tourist industry know that excessive development of seaside areas by the industry damages the environment. Such development also hurts the tourist industry by making these areas unattractive to tourists, a fact of which people in the tourist industry are well aware. People in the tourist industry would never knowingly do anything to damage the industry. Therefore, they would never knowingly damage the seaside environment, and people who are concerned about damage to the seaside environment thus have nothing to fear from the tourist industry.
questionStem: The reasoning in the argument is most vulnerable to criticism on which one of the following grounds?

answerChoices.A: No support is provided for the claim that excessive development hurts the tourist industry.
answerChoices.B: That something is not the cause of a problem is used as evidence that it never coexists with that problem.
answerChoices.C: The argument shifts from applying a characteristic to a few members of a group to applying the characteristic to all members of that group.
answerChoices.D: The possibility that the tourist industry would unintentionally harm the environment is ignored.
answerChoices.E: The argument establishes that a certain state of affairs is likely and then treats that as evidence that the state of affairs is inevitable.

stimulus:
Health officials claim that because the foods and beverages mentioned or consumed on many television programs are extremely low in nutritional
value, watching television has a bad influence on the dietary habits of television viewers.
questionStem: The claim by health officials depends on the presupposition that

answerChoices.A: the eating and drinking habits of people on television programs are designed to mirror the eating and drinking habits of television viewers
answerChoices.B: seeing some foods and beverages being consumed on, or hearing them mentioned on, television programs increases the likelihood that viewers will consume similar kinds of foods and beverages
answerChoices.C: the food and beverage industry finances television programs so that the foods and beverages that have recently appeared on the market can be advertised on those programs
answerChoices.D: television viewers are only interested in the people on television programs who have the same eating and drinking habits as they do
answerChoices.E: the eating and drinking habits of people on television programs provide health officials with accurate predictions about the foods and beverages that will become popular among television viewers

stimulus: In an effort to boost sales during the summer months, which are typically the best for soft-drink sales, Foamy Soda lowered its prices. In spite of this, however, the sales of Foamy Soda dropped during the summer months.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, contributes to reconciling the apparent discrepancy indicated above except:

answerChoices.A: The soft-drink industry as a whole experienced depressed sales during the summer months.
answerChoices.B: Foamy Soda’s competitors lowered their prices even more drastically during the summer months.
answerChoices.C: Because of an increase in the price of sweeteners, the production costs of Foamy Soda rose during the summer months.
answerChoices.D: A strike at Foamy Soda’s main plant forced production cutbacks that resulted in many stores not receiving their normal shipments during the summer months.
answerChoices.E: The weather during the summer months was unseasonably cool, decreasing the demand for soft drinks.

stimulus: Dr. Z: Many of the characterizations of my work offered by Dr. Q are imprecise, and such characterizations do not provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of my work.
questionStem: Which one of the following can be properly inferred from Dr. Z’s statement?

answerChoices.A: Some of Dr. Q’s characterizations of Dr. Z’s work provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of Dr. Z’s work.
answerChoices.B: All of Dr. Q’s characterizations of Dr. Z’s work that are not imprecise provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of Dr. Z’s work.
answerChoices.C: All of the characterizations of Dr. Z’s work by Dr. Q that do not provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of Dr. Z’s work are imprecise.
answerChoices.D: If the characterization of someone’s work is precise, then it provides a sound basis for criticizing that work.
answerChoices.E: At least one of Dr. Q’s characterizations of Dr. Z’s work fails to provide an adequate basis for sound criticism of that work.

stimulus: K, a research scientist, was accused of having falsified laboratory data. Although the original data in question have disappeared, data from K’s more recent experiments have been examined and clearly none of them were falsified. Therefore, the accusation should be dismissed.
questionStem: Which one of the following contains questionable reasoning that is most similar to that in the argument above?

answerChoices.A: L, an accountant, was charged with having embezzled funds from a client. The charge should be ignored, however, because although the records that might reveal this embezzlement have been destroyed, records of L’s current clients show clearly that there has never been any embezzlement from them.
answerChoices.B: M, a factory supervisor, was accused of failing to enforce safety standards. This accusation should be discussed, because although the identity of the accuser was not revealed, a survey of factory personnel revealed that some violations of the standards have occurred.
answerChoices.C: N, a social scientist, was charged with plagiarism. The charge is without foundation because although strong similarities between N’s book and the work of another scholar have been discovered, the other scholar’s work was written after N’s work was published.
answerChoices.D: O, an auto mechanic, has been accused of selling stolen auto parts. The accusation seems to be justified since although no evidence links O directly to these sales, the pattern of distribution of the auto parts points to O as the source.
answerChoices.E: P, a politician, has been accused of failing to protect the public interest. From at least some points of view, however, the accusation will undoubtedly be considered false, because there is clearly disagreement about where the public interest lies.

stimulus: The widespread staff reductions that accompanied the recent downturn in the economy were often accompanied by a fall in retail sales. A region’s retail sales depend on the spending power of people employed in the region: the more people employed, the greater are their resources, and so the higher are their expenditures. Since the downturn, employment has fallen. Therefore, retail sales have fallen because people are spending less than they used to.
questionStem: The argument in the passage proceeds by doing which one of the following?

answerChoices.A: drawing a causal conclusion on the basis of a correlation
answerChoices.B: inferring a general conclusion about a category of events from a single instance of that category
answerChoices.C: ruling out alternative explanations of a correlation
answerChoices.D: drawing a causal conclusion by appealing to general principles about the behavior of economic systems
answerChoices.E: inferring that changes in an aggregate must be explained by an individual’s actions

stimulus: The widespread staff reductions that accompanied the recent downturn in the economy were often accompanied by a fall in retail sales. A region’s retail sales depend on the spending power of people employed in the region: the more people employed, the greater are their resources, and so the higher are their expenditures. Since the downturn, employment has fallen. Therefore, retail sales have fallen because people are spending less than they used to.
questionStem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the argument relies?

answerChoices.A: people in the region who continue to be employed are not now paying off their debts at an accelerated rate
answerChoices.B: people in the region who continue to be employed are pessimistic about their prospects for continued employment
answerChoices.C: people in the region who have lost their jobs had no savings to draw on
answerChoices.D: retail sales depend on people’s expectations as well as on their current resources
answerChoices.E: people in the region who continue to be employed have debts they are paying off at an accelerated rate

stimulus: In Australia the population that is of driving age has grown larger over the last five years, but the annual number of traffic fatalities has declined. This leads to the conclusion that, overall, the driving-age population of Australia consists of more skillful drivers now than five years ago.
questionStem: Each of the statements below, if true, weakens the argument except:

answerChoices.A: Three years ago, a mandatory seat-belt law went into effect throughout Australia.
answerChoices.B: Five years ago, Australia began a major road repair project.
answerChoices.C: Because of increases in the price of fuel, Australians on average drive less each year than in the preceding year.
answerChoices.D: The number of hospital emergency facilities in Australia has doubled in the last five years.
answerChoices.E: In response to an increase in traffic fatalities, Australia instituted a program of mandatory driver education five years ago.

stimulus:
Anthropological studies indicate that distinct cultures differ in their moral codes. Thus, as long as
there are distinct cultures, there are no values shared across cultures.
questionStem: Each of the following, if true, would weaken the argument except:

answerChoices.A: Anthropologists rely on inadequate translation techniques to investigate the values of cultures that use languages different from the anthropologists’ languages.
answerChoices.B: As a result of advancing technology and global communication, we will someday all share the same culture and the same values.
answerChoices.C: Although specific moral values differ across cultures, more general moral principles, such as “Friendship is good,” are common to all cultures.
answerChoices.D: The anthropologists who have studied various cultures have been biased in favor of finding differences rather than similarities between distinct cultures.
answerChoices.E: What appear to be differences in values between distinct cultures are nothing more than differences in beliefs about how to live in accordance with shared values.

stimulus: Newspaper editor: Law enforcement experts, as well as most citizens, have finally come to recognize that legal prohibitions against gambling all share a common flaw: no matter how diligent the effort, the laws are impossible to enforce. Ethical qualms notwithstanding, when a law fails to be effective, it should not be a law. That is why there should be no legal prohibition against gambling.
questionStem: Which one of the following, if assumed, allows the argument’s conclusion to be properly drawn?

answerChoices.A: No effective law is unenforceable.
answerChoices.B: All enforceable laws are effective.
answerChoices.C: No legal prohibitions against gambling are enforceable.
answerChoices.D: Most citizens must agree with a law for the law to be effective.
answerChoices.E: Most citizens must agree with a law for the law to be enforceable.

stimulus: Copernicus’s astronomical system is superior to Ptolemy’s and was so at the time it was proposed, even though at that time all observational evidence was equally consistent with both theories. Ptolemy believed that the stars revolved around the earth at great speeds. This struck Copernicus as unlikely; he correctly thought that a simpler theory is that the earth rotates on its axis.
questionStem: The argument most closely conforms to which one of the following principles?

answerChoices.A: Simplicity should be the sole deciding factor in choosing among competing scientific theories.
answerChoices.B: If one theory is likely to be true, and another competing theory is likely to be false, then the one likely to be true is the superior of the two.
answerChoices.C: If all observational evidence is consistent with two competing theories, the one that is more intuitively true is the more practical theory to adopt.
answerChoices.D: Other things being equal, the more complex of two competing theories is the inferior theory.
answerChoices.E: Other things being equal, the simpler of two competing theories is the more scientifically important theory.

stimulus: Essayist: The existence of a moral order in the universe— i.e., an order in which bad is always eventually punished and good rewarded—depends upon human souls being immortal. In some cultures this moral order is regarded as the result of a karma that controls how one is reincarnated, in others it results from the actions of a supreme being who metes out justice to people after their death. But however a moral order is represented, if human souls are immortal, then it follows that the bad will be punished.
questionStem: Which one of the following most accurately describes a flaw in the essayist’s reasoning?

answerChoices.A: From the assertion that something is necessary to a moral order, the argument concludes that that thing is sufficient for an element of the moral order to be realized.
answerChoices.B: The argument takes mere beliefs to be established facts.
answerChoices.C: From the claim that the immortality of human souls implies that there is a moral order in the universe, the argument concludes that there being a moral order in the universe implies that human souls are immortal.
answerChoices.D: The argument treats two fundamentally different conceptions of a moral order as essentially the same.
answerChoices.E: The argument’s conclusion is presupposed in the definition it gives of a moral order.

stimulus: No mathematical proposition can be proven true by observation. It follows that it is impossible to know any mathematical proposition to be true.
questionStem: The conclusion follows logically if which one of the following is assumed?

answerChoices.A: Only propositions that can be proven true can be known to be true.
answerChoices.B: Observation alone cannot be used to prove the truth of any proposition.
answerChoices.C: If a proposition can be proven true by observation, then it can be known to be true.
answerChoices.D: Knowing a proposition to be true is impossible only if it cannot be proven true by observation.
answerChoices.E: Knowing a proposition to be true requires proving it true by observation.

stimulus:
Economist: In order to decide what to do about protecting the ozone layer, we must determine the monetary amount of the economic resources that we would willingly expend to protect it. Such a determination amounts to a calculation of the monetary value of the ozone layer. Environmentalists argue that the ozone layer does not have a calculable monetary value. However, we would not willingly expend an amount equal to all of the world’s economic resources to protect the ozone layer,
so the ozone layer is demonstrably worth less than that amount. Thus, the ozone layer has a calculable monetary value.
questionStem: The reasoning in the economist’s argument is flawed in that the argument

answerChoices.A: uses evidence that the monetary value of a particular natural resource is less than a certain amount in order to establish that the monetary value of any natural resource is less than that amount
answerChoices.B: presupposes that the ozone layer should not be protected and then argues to that claim as a conclusion
answerChoices.C: takes advantage of an ambiguity in the term “value” to deflect the environmentalists’ charge
answerChoices.D: gives no reason for thinking that merely establishing an upper limit on a certain monetary value would allow the calculation of that monetary value
answerChoices.E: does not directly address the argument of the environmentalists

stimulus: Columnist on the arts: My elected government representatives were within their rights to vote to support the arts with tax dollars. While funded by the government, however, some artists have produced works of art that are morally or aesthetically offensive to many taxpayers. Nonetheless, my conclusion is that no taxpayers have been treated unjustly whose tax dollars are used to fund some particular work of art that they may find abominable.
questionStem: Which one of the following principles, if valid, most supports the columnist’s argument?

answerChoices.A: Taxpayers should be allowed to decide whether a portion of their tax dollars is to be used to fund the arts.
answerChoices.B: The funding of a particular activity is warranted if it is funded by elected representatives who legitimately fund that activity in general.
answerChoices.C: Elected representatives are within their rights to fund any activity that is supported by a majority of their constituents.
answerChoices.D: Those who resent taxation to subsidize offensive art should vote against their incumbent government representatives.
answerChoices.E: Since taxpayers are free to leave their country if they disapprove of their representatives’ decisions, they have no right to complain about arts funding.
